issue_title: Make parameters metadata accessible through Python and the Web API
issue_body:
In a YAML parameter file, I can define some metadata, for instance a `unit` and a `reference`:

```YAML
description: Taux d'imposition maximal
reference: "http://legifrance/..."
unit: /1
values:
  2006-01-01:
    value: 0.6
  2007-01-01:
    value: 0.5
  2011-01-01:
    value: null
```

But these metadata are not accessible neither in Python, nor through the Web API.

-----

As a client of the API building a front displaying the parameters,
I can access the metadata through the Web API
So that I can format my parameters accordingly

There are currently a few places where inferences take place:
- In parameters values, through the `fuzzy` attribute, which artificially extends the validity period of a value — #437.
- In period management, through `calculate_output`, which can automatically sum or divide values over periods to match the requested period.
- In period management, through `set_input`, which can automatically sum or divide input values over periods to match the computable period.
- In some formulas, through `base_function`, which can yield values that the original requested formula could not compute on its own — #460.
- In some formulas, through `max_nb_cycles`, which can block the computation toward the past and thus doesn't allow some values to be computed.

We need to agree on a common, systematic way of doing inferences, to avoid conflicting views such as in #437 or in branch [`extrapolate-intensive-variables-to-past`](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-core/commits/extrapolate-intensive-variables-to-past).

The minimal output is a complete list of inferences and expected usage in the documentation, which could be based upon the above list.

The expected output is a set of helper functions, architectural changes and documentation that support a consistent way of inferring values in OpenFisca.

issue_title: GroupPicker fields cannot be read out like other fields.
issue_body:
I have a project where I have added a GroupPicker field to an issue. When I try to read the issue, I cannot (easily) access he name of the group listed:

```python
issue = jira.issue('SIS-11', fields = ['customfield_10800'])
print('issue', issue)

print('grouplist', issue.fields.customfield_10800)
print('group', issue.fields.customfield_10800[0])
print('group-name', issue.fields.customfield_10800[0]._session['name'])  # Weirdly works...

print('expected', issue.fields.customfield_10800[0].name)  # Boom!
```

yields (lines reordered):

```
issue SIS-11
grouplist [<JIRA Resource at 4402162936>]
group <JIRA Resource at 4402162936>
group-name jira-software-users
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "jira-python/jira/resources.py", line 160, in __getattr__
    return self[item]
TypeError: 'Resource' object is not subscriptable

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "jira-python/sample.py", line __, in <module>
    print('expected', issue.fields.customfield_10800[0].name)  # Boom!
  File "jira-python/jira/resources.py", line 173, in __getattr__
    if hasattr(self, 'raw') and item in self.raw:
TypeError: argument of type 'NoneType' is not iterable
```

The response of the REST call looks quite sensible:

```
GET http://jira:8080/rest/api/2/issue/SIS-11?os_authType=basic&fields=customfield_10800

{
  "expand": "renderedFields,names,schema,operations,editmeta,changelog,versionedRepresentations",
  "id": "10600",
  "self": "http://jira:8080/rest/api/2/issue/10600",
  "key": "SIS-11",
  "fields": {
    "customfield_10800": [
      {
        "name": "jira-software-users",
        "self": "http://jira:8080/rest/api/2/group?groupname=jira-software-users"
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

As far as I can see from debugging, the problem lies in creating a bland Resource in line 921. However, the constructor signature of Resource is different from those of the subclasses. Therefore, information is misplaced in the created object.

https://github.com/pycontribs/jira/blob/0243eac8570366170af98789e38c1a55c1c6eda3/jira/resources.py#L919-L928

https://github.com/pycontribs/jira/blob/0243eac8570366170af98789e38c1a55c1c6eda3/jira/resources.py#L963-L969

state.py: new_symbolic_buffer vs new_symbolic_value.
One uses the keyword argument `name` and the other `label` for the same thing

One of the major computational and memory efficiency bottlenecks in DyNet code is when the same operation is called multiple times within a for loop. This code can be sped up and made significantly more memory efficiency by moving that repetitive call outside the for loop.

Unfortunately many people don't realize this. It would be nice if DyNet had the ability to detect when people were doing the same operation multiple times, and re-use the results if so. The only difficulty is how to make this caching fast enough that it doesn't impact people adversely when they are already using optimized code. Perhaps we can add it as a flag?

issue_title: "Bad parameter specification" while reading some models
issue_body:
I believe that this happens when a model has some FULL_GRAD lines after some ZERO_GRAD lines.

I think the reason is in function "read_param_header", lines 41-53.

https://github.com/clab/dynet/blob/master/dynet/io.cc

called from line 157 of the same file. The logic here is such that "zero_grad" is initialized to "false" on line 147 (outside of the while loop), but once it is set to "true" by "read_param_header", there is no way for it to become "false" again, hence the bug (it breaks on trying to interpret a line of gradient values as a parameter header).

issue_title: Better support for saving/loading some, but not all parameters
issue_body:
Currently it is not easy to save/load only some of the parameters in the model. It would be nice to have an interface that made this easier.

This is partially related to: https://github.com/clab/dynet/issues/84

issue_title: The 'map_to_grid' algorithm with Barnes weighting includes masked elements in the result
issue_body:
When using the 'map_to_grid' algorithm with Barnes weighting, masked gates in the input radar may be used to interpolate a grid point.  
For example:

``` Python
radar = pyart.testing.make_target_radar()

fdata = radar.fields['reflectivity']['data']
fdata = np.ma.array(fdata)
fdata[100:120, 20:40] = -9999.
fdata[100:120, 20:40] = np.ma.masked
radar.fields['reflectivity']['data'] = fdata
print(radar.fields['reflectivity']['data'].min())

grids = pyart.map.map_to_grid(
    (radar, ),
    grid_shape=(3, 9, 10),
    grid_limits=((-400.0, 400.0), (-900.0, 900.0), (-900, 900)),
    roi_func=lambda z, y, x: 30,
    weighting_function='Barnes')
print(grids['reflectivity'].min())
```

The will print out 0.0 and -9999.0 indicating that the minimum value in the grid is below that minimum value in the radar which is not possible.

This is due to the numpy.ma.dot function not giving correct results.  Use of this function should be avoided.

Time with milliseconds is a bit overkill on plot titles, and takes extra space too. I propose these changes to drop milliseconds from plot titles:

`return num2date(times, units, calendar)` by `return num2date(times, units, calendar).replace(microsecond=0)` in functions `generate_radar_time_begin` (l126) and `generate_grid_time_begin` (l137) in `pyart/graph/common.py`

issue_title: Let NEXRADLevel2File's filename be a gzipped (.gz) file
issue_body:
All of the level 2 archive files that I've seen from [NOAA's data archive site](http://www.ncdc.noaa.gov/nexradinv/) have been .gz files.  Would it be worth detecting the filename's extension, and if it is a .gz file opening it with `gzip.open` instead of just using open?  As it is, you can work around this by using an open .gz file as the filename.  I can submit a pull request if I need to; it will basically only add a couple lines in `NEXRADLevel2File.__init__`:

    if filename.endswith('.gz'):  # maybe should use os.path.splitext()[-1] == '.gz' to be cross-platform?
        fh = gzip.open(filename, 'rb') # 'rb' is the default, don't really need it...

and it would need to add the relevant imports, too.

As an aside, everywhere I've seen on NOAA's website mentions that the radar data is stored as a bzipped file, but all of the ones I've seen have been gzipped.  Granted, I've only worked with a single data set from KTLX.  Are the others different?

@jjhelmus and @scollis from a past email I mentioned that the phase unwrap algorithm had difficulties with the insect and shallow cumulus layer, but I now have firm evidence that both the new algorithms have issues with this layer. Here's a [link](http://1drv.ms/1OjK7L4) to a full days worth of KAZR data that I have processed using `dealias_region_based`, where I have used the default settings. This shallow layer is incorrectly dealiased for about 20 hours, where at approximately 2033 UTC and afterwards the results improve. I think the cloud *feature* responsible for this sudden change is obvious and is almost certainly the reason for the poor results for times before 2033 UTC.

>>> Contains(MatchesDict({"a": "b"})).match([{"a": "b"}, {"b": "c"}])
None

issue_title: Check for general experimental data formats
issue_body: Take a look at FAIRDOM etc.
`plistlib` has been a built-in module since version 2.6. Also having it in there causes unexpected conflicts with `ufoLib`.

In `setup.py`, `install_requires` is filled with the content of [`requirements.txt`]( afdko/requirements.txt).
https://github.com/adobe-type-tools/afdko/blob/develop/setup.py#L205
https://github.com/adobe-type-tools/afdko/blob/develop/setup.py#L145-L147

These requirements dependency versions are pinned with version matching, which means some packages may be downgraded to those versions if more recent versions happen to be installed.

Could these be changed to inclusive ordered comparison `>=` or compatible release `~=` instead of version matching `==`?

As a suggestion, could we have more examples? Since this can get quite complicated, someone might just need the right example to get started.

I am also currently interested in this one over [here](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/42213153/access-lovoo-api-using-python), but also can't get it to work.

Most of the examples use oauth2, even though oauth1 is still very much in use.

For the APIs which support both REST and gRPC, how should we expose the choice?  One option would be to have a different `Connection` class for each endpoint:  if it works, then after instantiating the desired client, calling code would in theory be happy without caring again which one was chosen.  Thoughts?

Because the `datastore` API is protobufs-over-REST, its `Connection` class already has a bunch of domain-specific API calls already, which would make this plan simpler.  the `Connection` classes for `pubsub` and `logging` expose only `make_api_request`, which won't be much help.

issue_title: Project from client (bill-stackdriver-experiment) should agree with project from resource(1054618175867).
issue_body:
I'm not sure if this is a gcloud-python issue or a bug on the server-side.

Doing `list_log.entries()` gives the error:

```
  File "/Users/waprin/.virtualenvs/docsamples10/lib/python2.7/site-packages/gcloud/_helpers.py", line 464, in _name_from_project_path
    'project from resource(%s).' % (project, found_project))
ValueError: Project from client (bill-stackdriver-experiment) should agree with project from resource(1054618175867).
```

Adding some print statements reveals the reason:

`LOGGER NAME IS projects/1054618175867/logs/appengine.googleapis.com%2Frequest_log `

It seems some of the app engine logs return the project as the project number rather then the project ID. Note that 1054618175867 is the correct project number for the project. I do feel like there should be consistency and only the project id should be used so maybe this is a server side bug, but I'm not sure if there's any official external docs to reference. Getting the project number from a project id also might mean an extra API call is required just to do a validation. 

My vote would be to temporarily disable the validation for now and follow up internally about the format, thoughts?

issue_title: Remove pylint disable for too-many-lines in gcloud.streaming.transfer

issue_body:
Current plan of attack:

- [x] Remove `--plugin=protoc-gen-grpc=$(GRPC_PLUGIN)` from "helper" modules that don't use gRPC (e.g. `google.bigtable.v1.bigtable_data`) (this way sub-packages that don't need gRPC can use them) (#1354)
- [x] Ditch shared generated modules for those included with [`protobuf`][6] and [`googleapis-common-protos`][7] (#1353) ~~Move shared (i.e. non-Bigtable specific) modules from `gcloud.bigtable._generated` to `gcloud._generated` (this way all sub-packages can have access to shared protobuf message classes) and then subsequently update the `bigtable` imports to accommodate this change~~
- [x] Pull out [`_get_pb_property_value`][5] in `gcloud.bigtable.cluster` into core (`gcloud._helpers`) and separate out a `_has_field` piece since `pb_message.HasField` doesn't work on non-message fields in `proto3` (#1329)
- [x] Update `Makefile` to reflect changes above (i.e. make sure it can be run without changing the code) (See #1316)
- [x] Update `Makefile` to automatically add the `_foo.proto` files to the repo (See #1317)
- [x] Fold `Batch.add_auto_id_entity` into `Batch.put` (the `auto_id_entity` mutation is removed in favor of just using `insert` with a partial key) (see #1296)
- [x] Loosen `Batch` implementation's reliance on `Mutation` message class (it is used in a `LookupRequest` and their combined structure changes in `v1beta3`) (See #1306, #1319, #1341)
- [x] Remove use of `serializable` in `Client.transaction` and `Transaction` (it is no longer an option in `v1beta3`, so we will curtail it's use in `v1beta2` before the switch) (See #1294)
- [x] Manually partition the `_datastore_v1_pb2.py` module into 3 modules that just import portions of the namespace. These modules (`datastore_pb2.py`, `entity_pb2.py`, `query_pb2.py`) will be according to the membership in the new [proto definitions][4] for `v1beta3`. (See #1297, #1299, #1301, #1328)
- [x] Switch over imports from directly using `_datastore_v1_pb2.py` to using our "shim" imports to mock the structure of `v1beta3`. (See #1297, #1299, #1301, #1328)
- [x] Make sure `API_BASE_URL` for the `Connection` class is used (rather than a parent's version) (See #1293)
- [x] Change `Client.dataset_id` to `Client.project` (in advance of the rename to `PartitionId.project_id` in `v1beta3`) (#1330)
- [x] Change the return type of `Connection.commit` to be a tuple of `index_updates` and `mutation_results` (they were previously on the same result object but are being split apart in `v1beta3`) (See #1314)
- [x] Replace uses of `HasField` for non-message values with `_has_field` (see above) (#1329)
- [x] Remove `v1beta2` generated `pb2` file and old `.proto` definition (can also remove `_datastore_v1_pb2.py` from the `pylintrc_default` ignored file) and delete our "shim" modules (#1355, #1428)
- [x] Update `Makefile` to incorporate protobuf definitions for `v1beta3` (I did this in a [side-project][3], #1353, #1354, #1355, #1428)
- [x] ~~Rewrite our "shim" imports to use the actual `datastore._generated` files for `v1beta3`.~~
- [x] Switch `API_BASE_URL` from `https://www.googleapis.com` to `https://datastore.googleapis.com` and drop `'https://www.googleapis.com/auth/userinfo.email` from the scope list (#1339, #1406)
- [x] Accommodate renames / retyped / removed
    - [x] `CommitRequest.mutation  -->  CommitRequest.mutations` (#1461)
    - [x] `LookupRequest.key  -->  LookupRequest.keys` (~~#1358~~, #1456)
    - [x] `AllocateIdsResponse.key  -->  AllocateIdsResponse.keys` (~~#1358~~, #1456)
    - [x] `AllocateIdsRequest.key  -->  AllocateIdsRequest.keys` (~~#1358~~, #1456)
    - [x] `Key.path_element  -->  Key.path` (~~#1360~~, #1457)
    - [x] `Entity.property  -->  Entity.properties` (#1458)
    - [x] `Query.group_by  -->  Query.distinct_on` (~~#1357~~, #1455)
    - [x] `Query.limit  -->  Query.limit.value` (~~#1357~~, #1455)
    - [x] `QueryResultBatch.entity_result  -->  QueryResultBatch.entity_results` (~~#1357~~, #1455)
    - [x] `PartitionId.namespace  -->  PartitionId.namespace_id` (~~#1359~~, #1452)
    - [x] `PartitionId.dataset_id  -->  PartitionId.project_id` (~~#1359~~, #1452)
    - [x] `Value.indexed  -->  Value.exclude_from_indexes` (~~#1365~~, #1453)
    - [x] `Value.list_value  -->  Value.array_value` (#1460)
    - [x] `Value.null_value` added (#1464)
    - [x] `Value.timestamp_microseconds_value  -->  Value.timestamp_value` (with type change) (#1361, #1459)
    - [x] `Value.geo_point_value` added (#1464)
    - [x] `Value.blob_key_value` removed
    - [x] `CompositeFilter.filter  -->  CompositeFilter.filters` (~~#1356~~, #1454)
    - [x] `CompositeFilter.operation  -->  CompositeFilter.op` (~~#1356~~, #1454)
    - [x] `PropertyFilter.operation  -->  PropertyFilter.op` (~~#1356~~, #1454)
    - [x] `CompositeFilter.AND  -->  CompositeFilter.OPERATOR_UNSPECIFIED` (as default value)  (~~#1356~~, #1454)
    - [x] `ReadOptions.DEFAULT  -->  ReadOptions.READ_CONSISTENCY_UNSPECIFIED` (as default value)  (~~#1356~~, #1454)
- [x] Deal with the fact that `Entity.property` is not a `map` called `Entity.properties` (somewhat different semantics) (#1340)
- [x] Tear out dataset (project) [prefix code][8] (#1466)
- [x] Re-vamp environment variable usage (`dataset_id` no longer needed?) (#1465)
- [x] Remove use of `isolation_level` in `Connection.begin_transaction` (#1343, #1407)

New Features:

- [ ] Add `GqlQuery` support (#304)
- [ ] `QueryResultBatch.skipped_cursor` added (only set when `skipped_results != 0`)
- [ ] `EntityResult.cursor` added (set by the backend when the entity result is part of a `RunQueryResponse.batch.entity_results` response)
- [ ] Parse protobuf [errors][9]

[4]: https://github.com/google/googleapis/tree/12dd2eafc6ba6d011c4079cbfb246b60f9fdc28d/google/datastore/v1beta3
[5]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/94c007dea5a2080cd4dfef0c8d62a6c30d82000e/gcloud/bigtable/cluster.py#L50-L71
[6]: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/protobuf
[7]: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/googleapis-common-protos
[8]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/3f189536262a7f2046a66c5fb0cb9487ecb0269c/gcloud/datastore/helpers.py#L421
[9]: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/pull/1466#issuecomment-183822889

issue_title: Implement multi-row mutations for python Bigtable
issue_labels: [{'id': 210703442, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/api:%20bigtable', 'name': 'api: bigtable', 'color': 'cd7f32', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604243, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/priority:%20p2+', 'name': 'priority: p2+', 'color': 'ffee58', 'default': False}, {'id': 77438582, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/type:%20enhancement', 'name': 'type: enhancement', 'color': '8bc34a', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
It would be great if you could add multi-row mutations to the Bigtable python client library. AFAICS it doesn't exist yet.

The RPC API supports it:
https://cloud.google.com/bigtable/docs/reference/data/rpc/google.bigtable.v2#mutaterowsrequest

It just isn't implemented for python. The first use case I ran into is I have a bunch of rows to delete and a want to make a single RPC to do that, but I'm sure there will be more.

issue_title: How should we pass options for http clients in service client creation?
issue_body:
In order to address #2988, where timeouts are not a service option, but need to be handled by the HTTP client.

How should we handle passing arguments like `timeout` when creating a service client?

```python
client = someservice.Client(timeout=10)
```

This value could then be passed [here](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/blob/14c250316aeef72b7f951a0959c46855948bd2b7/core/google/cloud/client.py#L118-L139) but we would need to assume the argument was available.

In this case [`google_auth_httplib2.AuthorizedHttp`](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-auth-library-python/blob/d840beceafacd37a6abf993cd1fb8039eb3b6864/httplib2_transport/google_auth_httplib2.py#L147) doesn't take the argument but rather an already created http client.

We could make another mixin class for `Client` I suppose that supports setting `timeout`?

issue_body:
Inserting rows turned out to be very slow (80 KB/s), so I am trying multiprocessing to parallelize the process.
I am currently using only one node in Cloud Spanner, the rows are sorted by primary key and there are ~30 batches that have a few hundred rows each.
For this I am using the following code:
```
pool = Pool(5)
pool.starmap(_process_batch, batches)


def _process_batch(instance_name, database_name, table_name, column_names, b):
    spanner_client = spanner.Client(credentials=get_credentials())
    instance = spanner_client.instance(instance_name)
    database = instance.database(database_name)
    with database.batch() as batch:
        batch.insert(table=table_name, columns=tuple(column_names), values=b)
```
The code hangs when it tries to enter the batch scope (it seems to never succeed in creating a session).
Any ideas?

issue_title: Storage: add support for Per-Object Storage Class
issue_labels: [{'id': 77678602, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/API:%20storage', 'name': 'API: storage', 'color': 'c0c0c0', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604252, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/Priority:%20P0', 'name': 'Priority: P0', 'color': 'db4437', 'default': False}, {'id': 486182624, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/Status:%20Release%20Blocking', 'name': 'Status: Release Blocking', 'color': 'ffee58', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
We need to add support for Per-Object Storage Class (POSC).

Here some extra information to help:

- [POSC info from Cloud Storage documentation](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/per-object-storage-class)
- [POSC example with JSON API](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/changing-storage-classes)
- [Storage Class info from Cloud Storage documentation](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/storage-classes)

On top of that, google-cloud-dotnet has made an implementation which can be used as reference. 

- [Please view the Pull Request to understand the changes](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-dotnet/pull/766)

If you have any implementation questions, please direct them at jskeet

/cc @jskeet @danoscarmike

I hope I am not misunderstanding the availability of the `dry_run` parameter as [listed on the docs](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/stable/bigquery-job.html#google.cloud.bigquery.job.QueryJob.dry_run). google-api-python-client==1.5.5

google-cloud-bigquery==0.22.0
google-cloud-core==0.22.1
google-cloud-storage==0.22.0
googleapis-common-protos==1.5.0
```

4. Stacktrace if available
NIL

5. Steps to reproduce
- create job with run_async_query()
- set job.dry_run = True
- run it with job.begin()
- examine job when it is done

```
job = client.run_async_query(JOB_NAME, QUERY_STRING)

job.begin()
print job.__dict__

issue_title: Replace usage of to_string_lossy in Path
issue_body: Rather than using `to_string_lossy` when creating python `Path` objects from rust, we should likely make the `store_utf8` method faliable, and then let python do the decoding of the path bytes (which might possibly fail).

issue_title: The Cobertura plugin includes a slf4j implementation on the classpath
issue_body:
The Pants Cobertura plugin includes `slf4j-simple` into the runtime classpath; this causes some of our tests to fail due to slf4j binding to the wrong implementation.

Removing the implementation from the Cobertura runtime classpath resolves this issue for me; I've also removed our slf4j implementation from the classpath and the coverage still runs without error.
```
    register_jvm_tool(register,
                      'cobertura-run',
                      classpath=[
                        cobertura_jar(intransitive=True),
                        #slf4j_jar
                      ])
```
I've also been able to remove it from the instrumentation runtime and I've not seen any errors (although leaving it in place doesn't cause a problem for us).
```
    register_jvm_tool(register,
                      'cobertura-instrument',
                      classpath=[
                        cobertura_jar(),
                        #slf4j_jar
                      ])
```

issue_title: Memoize ScalaPlatform _tool_classpath invocations and store a DirectoryDigest from them
issue_body:
Rather than relying on consumers capturing and memoizing as they want.

We need to make sure that paths are properly relativised.

Currently [JvmdocGen](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/tasks/jvmdoc_gen.py) relies on the `scaladoc` and `javadoc` commands to be on the user's `PATH` and does not validate this and fail gracefully when they are not.  The task should be improved to do so - or better - to bootstrap the tool / run it from a classpath as a standard jvm tool.

issue_title: ids created by Address.path_safe_spec() are not unique
issue_body:
This came up in a conversation in https://rbcommons.com/s/twitter/r/2021

If a target is defined with a '.' in the name, you could get collisions. E.g. the specs:

```
foo/bar:baz
foo:bar.baz
```
both map to

```
foo.bar.baz
```
when you run them through Address.path_safe_spec()

One solution is to transform the ':' into another character that is legal in a filename but not legal in a target name. I'm not sure what that would be though.

Another solution would be to make '.' illegal in a target name.

issue_title: Remove the \ufeff character from any file that is read?
issue_body:
This is the 10th time that I had these issues: reading in a file, searching for a header, I see an error, and only in the end I find out, why the header (in this case "ID") was not found: The first character was ```\ufeffID```.

This happened in the concepticon-api, which uses clldutils, and I wonder, given that this usually leads to a long debugging session, whether we should not remove this character immediately if it occurs as the header? Or are there other preferred ways how to deal with it?

issue_title: Update documentation and/or error messaging around lack of support for conditions
issue_body:
It seems as if conditions are not supported in all or portions of AWS::Serverless::Function (and perhaps other resource types, but I haven't personally experimented).

Searching this repo has shown that this is in fact a current feature request shown here -
 https://github.com/awslabs/serverless-application-model/issues/142

Until such feature is available, it may be useful to update the documentation to include this lack of support.  Currently inclusion of a condition in such function definition leads to a generic "internal transform failure" error message this is not surfaced until one tries to create a change set (not caught in template validation).

Updating the documentation and/or error messaging may be useful in the interim time until such condition support is available to help others avoid the laborious task of trying to troubleshoot these types of issues in their templates.

issue_title: satosa-saml-metadata creates <EntitiesDescriptor> for a single entity
issue_body:
I got a report that satosa-saml-metadata always creates the root element <EntitiesDescriptor> even if there is only one Entity. This apparently breaks the metadata for some implementations.

https://docs.oasis-open.org/security/saml/v2.0/saml-metadata-2.0-os.pdf
2.3 Root Elements A SAML metadata instance describes either a single entity or multiple entities. In the former case, the root element MUST be <EntityDescriptor> In the latter case, the root element MUST be <EntitiesDescriptor>

issue_title: DataItem 110 "Height Measured by a 3D Radar": Height is signed not unsigned
issue_body:
At least in version 1.21 of the CAT048 specification the DataItem 110 "Height Measured by a 3D Radar" is signed not unsigned: "3D height, in binary notation. Negative values are expressed in two’s complement."

So

`<Bits from="14" to="1">`

should be

`<Bits from="14" to="1" encode="signed">`

issue_title: `charm proof` should check that referenced terms are released
issue_body:
A charm that references unreleased or non-existent terms in its metadata.yaml cannot be released.

Therefore I recommend that the `charm proof` tool check this, for charms that use terms.

The API call is simple:

`https://api.jujucharms.com/terms/v1/terms/{owner}/{term-name}` yields a list of all revisions for the term {term-name} owned by {owner}. Add `?revision={rev-no}` for a specific revision.

`https://api.jujucharms.com/terms/v1/terms/{term-name}` does the same for curated term IDs that aren't managed by an owner.

An example response is here: https://api.jujucharms.com/terms/v1/terms/canonical

You'll want to check for `{"published": true}` on the resulting object matching the charm's metadata. If none exists, then the charm cannot be deployed and `charm proof` should at least warn.

issue_title: The websocket connection can fail silently
issue_body:
I don't have a clean repro yet, but I believe this to be the underlying cause of this bug in matrix: https://github.com/juju-solutions/matrix/issues/92

Basically, matrix periodically checks on the "health" of a cluster by checking the status for each unit in each application in Model.applications, where Model is the Model class from python-libjuju's model.py. That data is supposed to be updated by a listener inside the Model object.

If matrix is running on a flaky connection, or if the Controller times the websocket connection out, the listener will stop receiving updates, however, with no Exceptions being thrown. I've verified that this is what's happening by running matrix in a debugger, and checking the .is_open property of the connection. Eventually, it gets set to false because we have been disconnected from the Controller.

We need to either come up with an automagic reconnection scheme, or a recommended pattern for dealing with the case where the listener gets disconnected. 

Note that this is semi-related to https://github.com/juju/python-libjuju/issues/98, though making a Pinger would only address Controller initiated timeouts; a flaky connection would still kill the listener.

When you have a model like so:

```python
class Person(Model):
    id = columns.UUID(primary_key=True, default=uuid.uuid4)
    name = columns.Text(db_field="person_name", index=True)
```

and run `Person.objects.filter(name="someone")`, it results in:

```
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'primary_key'
```

This is due to `_validate_select_where()` not using the `db_field` name to get the column. (on line 709 in query.py).

issue_title: relaxation on normal equations and normal residual equations
issue_body:
Few things:

- There is a bug somewhere in get_diagonal(A, norm_eq=2), because in practice this is not equivalent to get_diagonal(A.T*A, norm_eq=1). This causes jacobi_ne, gaussseidel_ne, etc. to not work properly.

- Somehow I see different behavior in the amg_core C function for jacobi_ne vs. a pure python implementation. I believe the iteration in python should be 
    for i in range(0,iterations):
        res = A.T * (b - A*x)
        temp =  x + Dinv * res
where Dinv is Dinv(A^TA), but this returns different stuff than the amg_core version.

- For gaussseidel_ne and jacobi_ne : I assume "ne" stands for normal equations. But, in the descriptions, the normal equations are not used, the normal residual equations are. The normal equations are given by A^TAx = A^Tb, and the normal residual equations are AA^Ty = b, and x = A^Ty. What is actually happening in the code? Need to make sure descriptions and function names are accurate.

issue_title: calling nidcpower measure_multiple causes python to crash
issue_body:
Calling nidcpower measure_multiple causes python environment to crash when doing pytest.

```
import nidcpower
import pytest
import re


@pytest.fixture(scope='function')
def session():
    with nidcpower.Session('FakeDevice', '', False, 'Simulate=1, DriverSetup=Model:4143; BoardType:PXIe') as simulated_session:
        yield simulated_session


def test_measure_multiple(session):
    session.source_mode = nidcpower.SourceMode.SINGLE_POINT
    '''session.configure_aperture_time(0, nidcpower.ApertureTimeUnits.SECONDS)'''
    #session.voltage_level = 1
    count = 10
    #session.measure_when = nidcpower.MeasureWhen.ON_DEMAND
    with session.initiate():
        voltage_measurements, current_measurements = session.measure_multiple()
    assert len(voltage_measurements) == 4   # measuremultiple will return a reading for all channel , since 4143 has 4 channel expecting 4 readings 
    assert len(current_measurements) == 4 
    assert isinstance(voltage_measurement[1], float)  
    assert isinstance(current_measurements[1], float) 

```
Also noticed that assertion on test_get_attribute_string fails sometime when it is expecting 4143  session.instrument_model is returning as 4110.[Failure was consistent and disappeared after reboot]

In C API, customizing fetch is done by setting some properties. This is hidden and poorly understood. Michael's proposal below makes the API more discoverable and logical. His comments below:

In NI-SCOPE, an obvious example of this is our family of Fetch functions. The prototype for niScope_Fetch is:

```
ViStatus niScope_Fetch(
        ViSession vi,
        ViConstString channelList,
        ViReal64 timeout,
        ViInt32 numSamples,
        ViReal64* wfm,
        struct niScope_wfmInfo* wfmInfo);
```
But, in addition to that, the following attributes all impact what that function does:

* NISCOPE_ATTR_FETCH_RELATIVE_TO
* NISCOPE_ATTR_FETCH_OFFSET
* NISCOPE_ATTR_FETCH_RECORD_NUMBER
* NISCOPE_ATTR_FETCH_NUM_RECORDS     

Beyond not being very easy for users to discover (seriously, our documentation [1] doesn't even mention them), having to change the session state in order to change function behavior makes it very difficult to reason about the code and very easy to introduce subtle bugs that are difficult to track down.
 
The Python API inherited this. The signature of niscope.Session.fetch is:

```
def fetch(self, num_samples, timeout):
```

and users are required to somehow just know to set fetch_num_records in order to fetch more than 1 record from the device.

We can do better than that. A simple improvement would be to do something like:

```
# Defaults to fetching all samples from all records relative to
# pretrigger with a 10 second timeout.

def fetch(self, *kwargs, num_samples=-1, timeout=10.0,
          offset=0, record=0, num_records=-1,
          relative_to=enums.RelativeTo.PRETRIGGER):
```

where the function grabs a lock, saves the state of the fetch attributes, sets the fetch attributes based on the arguments, invokes fetch, and then restores fetch attributes to their prior values before returning. This makes the functionality of fetch more discoverable, and it doesn't change the session state.
 
[ Well, it's not 100% true that it doesn't change the session state because of the read pointer, but I've got a solution for that which I'm omitting for brevity, though I'd be happy to elaborate on it if anyone's interested ].

issue_title: Incorporate NI-SCOPE system tests into NI internal Automated Test Suite
issue_body:
NI maintains an Automated Test Suite for the driver.
It needs to install the corresponding nimi-python package and run the corresponding system tests as part of its execution.

This way we can ensure that future versions of the driver work with nimi-python.
It would be handy to have a multikey dict to use in various places for pkgcore, e.g. creating a better aliased mapping for portage-2 profile support.

apparantly, wheel isn't compatible with py 3.3 anymore .. https://travis-ci.org/crossbario/txaio/jobs/384048129

either we work around that (pinning, or other); or we deprecate py 3.3 also ..

The documentation [states](http://txaio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#txaio.add_callbacks):

> Note that `txaio` doesn’t do anything special with regards to callback or errback chaining – it is highly recommended that you always return the incoming argument unmodified in your callback/errback so that Twisted and asyncio behave the same.

But even for asyncio there is a slightly hacky call-chaining implemented in [txaio/aio.py](https://github.com/crossbario/txaio/blob/2cac68bd2b3a9ffbb984e08eb37f367a4cb0e462/txaio/aio.py#L375). And this seems to be used extensively, e.g. in [autobahn/wamp/protocol.py](https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/master/autobahn/wamp/protocol.py).

Please either update the documentation, rendering this functionality "official and supported" or remove the hack from aio.py (which I expect to cause quite some test failures in dependent libraries). If the decision is to remove the hack, please consider providing a replacement for this idiom used in [crossbario/autobahn-python](https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python):

    d = txaio.as_future(func1)
    txaio.add_callbacks(d, txaio.as_future(func2), None)
    txaio.add_callbacks(d, txaio.as_future(func3), None)

issue_title: using components transport.endpoint.tls.trust_root requires to call tls.load_default_certs() to work with sslmate certificate
issue_body:
We have a certificate generated with sslmate.

crosbar config:
```
---
version: 2
workers:
  - type: router
    realms:
      - name: foobar
        roles:
          - name: anonymous
            permissions:
              # allow all
              - uri: ""
                match: prefix
                allow:
                  call: true
                  register: true
                  publish: true
                  subscribe: true
                disclose:
                  caller: false
                  publisher: false
                cache: true
    transports:
      - type: websocket
        endpoint:
          type: tcp
          port: 8443
          tls:
            key: ../certs/foobar.key
            certificate: ../certs/foobar.crt
        url: wss://foobar:8443/ws
```

The autobahn component gets the following config (parsed from yaml and put straight into `**kwargs`):
```
component:
  realm: foobar
  transports:
    - type: websocket
      url: wss://foobar:8443/ws
      endpoint:
        type: tcp
        host: foobar
        port: 8443
        tls:
          hostname: foobar
          trust_root: certs/foobar.chain.crt
```

Connecting the component gives following error:
```
2018-05-29T08:56:54.330776Z [info     ] Connection failed: SSLError: [SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED] certificate verify failed (_ssl.c:833) [autobahn.asyncio.component.Component]
```


`openssl s_client -CAfile certs/foobar.chain.crt -showcerts -connect foobar:8443` works as expected.


When I call `tls.load_default_certs()` after https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/blob/master/autobahn/asyncio/component.py#L248 everything works.

So the question appears, is this intended and should I use a custom SSLContext for this, while I imaging it would be a good way to load default certs at least as an option, if not allways...

issue_title: remove pin on Twisted<=16.1.1, all versions (including current 16.2.0) are ok
issue_body:
A couple of weeks ago, in reponse to #641, setup.py acquired an `extras_require[twisted]` that pins the Twisted version:

```
    "Twisted>=15.5,<=16.1.1"        # MIT license
```

At the time, Twisted-16.1.1 was current, and Twisted trunk had committed a change which broke autobahn pretty badly (https://twistedmatrix.com/trac/ticket/8191 , landed on 29-Mar-2016 in twisted git revision 4f9e36d8ad21f9d73369f4d506bab30f61c2ad71). We were worried that the next version (16.1.2? 16.2.0?) would include the change, breaking the combination of (most-recent-Twisted-release) and (most-recent-Autobahn-release). So the pin froze the acceptable version of Twisted to the current release at that time (16.1.1), in anticipation of a broken release.

On 23-Apr-2016, Twisted reverted that change, in commit d03fee6d464a651e27f86836b15444d5b47bc734. The breaking change never made it into a release, and Twisted-16.2.0 was released on 17-May-2016 with no problems. All versions of Twisted (16.1.0, 16.1.1, 16.2.0) work with autobahn (at least w.r.t. the #641 web.Resource issue).

I'm wondering if autobahn could remove the pin. I have a project that needs both Autobahn and Twisted, and since pip doesn't have a full dependency resolver, running my `pip install magic-wormhole` gets me Twisted-16.2.0 and autobahn-0.14.0, and then my commands fail because the installed versions don't meet the pin requirements (under setuptools-21.2.1):

```
(ve4) % wormhole --version
Traceback (most recent call last):
 ...
"ve4/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pkg_resources/__init__.py", line 834, in resolve
    raise VersionConflict(dist, req).with_context(dependent_req)
pkg_resources.ContextualVersionConflict: (Twisted 16.2.0 (/Users/warner/stuff/tahoe/magic-wormhole/ve4/lib/python2.7/site-packages), Requirement.parse('Twisted<=16.1.1,>=15.5; extra == "twisted"'), set(['autobahn']))
```

To allow my users to run `pip install magic-wormhole` successfully, I'd need to pin twisted back myself. I'll do that if necessary (I'm trying to make a 1.0 release in time for my pycon presentation, and I want it to be installable by then), but it will make downstream packaging more difficult, and I think it'd be cleaner all around if the autobahn `setup.py` removed the constraint, now that it turned out to be unnecessary.

thanks!
 -Brian

issue_title: Overriding environment variables causes them to be interpreted as a string
issue_body:
From @tusharkhot 

Michael Schmitz So there is one issue with using environment variables as fallback in HOCON. These environment variables are assumed to be strings by default which lead to incompatible type errors in AllenNLP. E.g. when setting cuda_device using environment variables

```
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/allennlp/training/trainer.py", line 515, in from_params
    if cuda_device >= 0:
TypeError: '>=' not supported between instances of 'str' and 'int'
```

I can submit a PR to allennlp to convert every parameter to the right type (or at least the interesting ones) or maybe there is a fancy way to ensure environment variables have the right type ?

### What is wrong?
For sharding, we switch all `HexaryTrie` to `BinaryTrie`.

### How can it be fixed
Most changes are in `evm.db.chain.BaseChainDB`. I suggest we divide it into two parts:
1. `master` branch:
    * Make `trie_class` to be configurable in `BaseChainDB.__init__`.
    * Default: `HexaryTrie`

2. Rebase `sharding` branch and switch sharding-related tries to `BinaryTrie`.

issue_title: ChainSyncer: add tx lookup entries to DB
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

Currently (when doing a fast sync) it generates the transactions trie manually (using `make_trie_root_and_nodes()`) and persists that to the DB, so we don't get the DB entries that allow us to lookup the block of a given tx by hash.

### How can it be fixed

We could redesign `ChainSyncer` so that it uses `ChainDB.persist_block()`, but that would certainly affect performance significantly because that trie generation would be done in the DB process and that is quite expensive.

Another option would be to duplicate (or refactor) parts of `persist_block()` so that `ChainSyncer` calls `ChainDB._add_transaction_to_canonical_chain()` after it calls `ChainDB.persist_header()`

issue_title: Handle pending transactions with a tx pool
issue_body:
* Version: 0.1.0-beta.16
* Python: all
* OS: all


### What was wrong?

Eth tester does not handle pending transactions correctly when disabling auto mining transactions:

Sending a transaction with the same nonce causes an invalid nonce error:

```
from eth_tester import (
    EthereumTester,
    PyEVMBackend,
)

backend = PyEVMBackend()
t = EthereumTester(backend=backend)
t.disable_auto_mine_transactions()
accounts = t.get_accounts()
tx = t.send_transaction({'from': accounts[0], 'to': accounts[1], 'gas': 21000, 'value': 1, 'nonce': 0})
tx2 = t.send_transaction({'from': accounts[0], 'to': accounts[1], 'gas': 21000, 'value': 1, 'nonce': 0})
>>> evm.exceptions.ValidationError: Invalid transaction nonce
```
### How can it be fixed?

The issue is that `send_transaction` and `send_raw_transaction` immediately execute on the vm's state and thereby increment the valid nonce.

### Things to consider for the fix:

- A tx pool needs to be managed at the eth tester level
- It should consider the rules for replacing transactions. e.g. transactions with existing nonce should overwrite #57 
- Will the tx pool work for every backend? 
- `eth-tester` exposes both `send_transaction` and `send_raw_transaction` both of which have different code paths. We need to ensure that we treat them the same as far as the tx pool is concerned.  
- `eth-tester` does not know about the outcome of a transaction (e.g. for figuring out what nonce a transaction will execute at). Could circumvent this by using `get_transaction_by_hash` after executing the transaction.

A very rudimentary fix: 
```
if self.auto_mine_transactions:
    self.mine_block()
else:
    snapshot = self.take_snapshot()
    # If a pending transaction with the same nonce exists, overwrite it?
    self._pending_transactions.append(transaction)
    self.revert_to_snapshot(snapshot)

def enable_auto_mine_transactions(self):
    self.auto_mine_transactions = True
    return [self.send_transaction(tx) for tx in self._pending_transactions]
```

@carver @pipermerriam feel free to modify the issue to clarify things 🙂

### What was wrong?

It would be nice to be able to test chain-reorg in a simple manner. I'm not sure where in the stack the fits best but something like
```py
EthereumTester.mine_reorg(reorg_depth=1)
```
might work.

Main use case would be testing scenarios where state is updated from block contents and has to be updated when the chain changes.

issue_title: Activation script alter $IFS, hence altering for loops in bash
issue_body:
Hello Conda Devs!  

Great package overall, just a potential issue I've spotted in my own usage.  Basically, the IFS variable is set in whenever `source activate <env>` is called.  I think the error occurs here, [in line 95 the activation script on the current head.](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/97248c5a8b5c84c35415530fd6d7258e2050a2cd/shell/activate#L95)

This causes whitespace delimination to no longer work in bash `for` loops.  I have not checked the other shells.  

To recreate, verify that before any activation is done, the following command has the default behaviour in bash : 
```bash
bash$ for p in `echo hello:there:you:bug | sed 's/:/ /g'`; do echo $p; done
hello
there
you
bug
```

To trigger the bug, try creating a minimal environment with conda (python is used here, but any package should work), and insert an extra activation script in the correct directory: 
```bash
bash$ conda create -n foo python=2.7
Fetching package metadata .......
Solving package specifications: ..........
Using Anaconda Cloud api site https://api.anaconda.org

Package plan for installation in environment <install_prefix>/anaconda2/envs/foo:

The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    openssl:    1.0.2h-1     
    pip:        8.1.2-py27_0 
    python:     2.7.12-1     
    readline:   6.2-2        
    setuptools: 25.1.6-py27_0
    sqlite:     3.13.0-0     
    tk:         8.5.18-0     
    wheel:      0.29.0-py27_0
    zlib:       1.2.8-3      

Proceed ([y]/n)? y

Linking packages ...
[      COMPLETE      ]|################################################################################################################################################################| 100%
#
# To activate this environment, use:
# $ source activate foo
#
# To deactivate this environment, use:
# $ source deactivate
#
```

Activate the environment, create the directory for the extra activation scripts, write a dummy shell script to be sourced at line 95 of the main activation script, hence triggering the IFS bug on next activation.
```bash
bash$ source activate foo
bash$ mkdir -p ${CONDA_PREFIX}/etc/conda/activate.d
bash$ echo 'echo bar' > ${CONDA_PREFIX}/etc/conda/activate.d/fooActivate.sh
bash$ source deactivate
```

Reactivate the environment to trigger IFS related bug:
```bash
bash$ source activate foo
bar
bash$ for p in `echo hello:there:you:bug | sed 's/:/ /g'`; do echo $p; done
hello there you bug 
```

So, now IFS is actually set to something, so whitespace delimination is now broken in for loops.  This is especially harmful to any script that loops through the PATH variable in the above fashion.

issue_title: Windows 10 - SSL Error only solved when installing python from python.org
issue_body:
Guys,
My set up: Win10 + miniconda with python 3.5.2 + PyCharm 2016.2.2
I have had strange issue when I was trying to use quandl package. The error was 
urllib.error.URLError: <urlopen error [SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED] certificate verify failed (_ssl.c:645)>

code triggering this issue was : 
import Quandl as qd
ds = qd.get("FMAC/HPI_PA", authtoken="MyAuthTockenHere")
strange thing was that the same code was working on my old laptop on the same Windows 10 + miniconda and PyCharm installation. I've tried everything from Windows settings to miniconda and pycharm reinstallation along with all necessary packages update. Nothing worked only until I realised that on my old laptop I had Python 3.5.2 from python.org installed first before I moved to miniconda. So I installed Python 3.5 from Python.org and all started working immediately. 
Note that I have not even changed default project interpreter in PyCharm from miniconda python.exe to python.org python.exe it was just the fact I've installed it and this solved my issue.
Should this be working like this ? Does miniconda requires python installation from python.org or this is a bug ?
Regards
E

issue_title: version inference seems to assume .0 (zero) instead of .* (anything)
issue_body:
I cannot definitively tell if this is the case or not, but it appears that when I do something like:

```
conda install bokeh==0.12
```

And don't specify a patch version that Conda automatically assumes `.0`, IOW the statement above is equivalent to:

```
conda install bokeh==0.12.0
```

I would suggest that this is surprising to the end user who I would think, if they haven't specified a patch version, is expecting the latest patch version in the 0.12 release series.  IOW they are expecting that the first statement is equivalent instead to:

```
conda install bokeh==0.12.*
```

But perhaps there is another way to think about this that I am not aware of.

It is an aside, but I should say that I think the "wildcard" syntax that I see of `==0.12*` rather surprising.  Normal regex rules would suggest that such an expression would equivalently match `==0.129`, which surely Conda wouldn't do, but on the surface it makes one worry that it might.

issue_title: Error when using a conda environment file containing packages sourced from non-default channels
issue_body:
I've been following the [documentation page](http://conda.pydata.org/docs/using/envs.html) on exporting and using an environment file and have run into a problem. I have an active conda environment with dependencies from the [bioconda channel](https://anaconda.org/bioconda), the default conda channel, as well as pip dependencies, and I'm trying to create a conda environment file that encapsulates all installed packages, both pip and (bio)conda. Exporting an environment file lists everything I have installed. 

When I try to create a new environment from the environment file, I receive an error that the bioconda-sourced packages are missing. Are environment files not intended to install packages from other channels? I assumed because of the bioconda-prefix "namespace notation" that the packages would be sourced from the appropriate channel.

issue_title: "No such file or directory" during multiple parallel activations
issue_body:
Nearly identically to #2837, I am also working in a clustered environment where multiple parallel processes are activating the same env simultaneously and I am getting "No such file or directory" regularly.

In fact running the following script, in 3 different terminals at once, will reproduce the problem within a few seconds:

```
#!/bin/bash
# Look for race condition in activating an anaconda env
# Run this in multiple windows at once and wait for one of them to crash.

ret=0
while [ "$ret" == "0" ]; do
    echo -n .
    source activate myenv
    ret=$?
done
```

The immediate solution (as #2837 did) would appear to be to add another exception to the exception [in install.py in symlink_conda_hlp at ~line 616](https://github.com/kalefranz/conda/blob/8b5bc4ec76075f2896d5a53d1b5e41a0194a9c11/conda/install.py#L616).  This time adding errno.ENOENT to the list.

So many exceptions to exceptions raises the question in my mind if re-symlinking is the right thing to do during activation.   If the symlinks can't be entirely removed, could this be replaced with just a check if the symlinks are there and a warning if somehow they've gone missing?  Keeping it read-only will avoid these race conditions.  (In fact, the above suggestion probably doesn't remove all the possible race conditions here.)  An arg to activate to force re-symlinking could be added to override the default of not re-symlinking.  Just some thoughts...

CC'ing NERSC admins who have helped me debug this: @rcthomas

issue_body:
under my anaconda [channel](https://anaconda.org/mingfeima/mkldnn), the available versions for mkldnn is 
```bash
Loading channels: done
# Name                  Version           Build  Channel
mkldnn                   0.12.1               0  mingfeima
mkldnn                   0.13.0               0  mingfeima
mkldnn                   0.14.0               0  mingfeima
``` 
but when i install the library with
```bash
conda install -c mingfeima mkldnn 
```
conda is installing 0.13.0 instead of 0.14.0, like the following.
```bash
Solving environment: done

## Package Plan ##

  environment location: /home/mingfeim/anaconda3

  added / updated specs:
    - mkldnn


The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    mkldnn: 0.13.0-0 mingfeima
```
this is possibly related to [#786](https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/786)

since `mkldnn-v0.13` has dependency on `mkl-v2018.0.1` and `mkldnn-v0.14` has dependency on `mkl-v2018.0.3`, if i have `mkl-v2018.0.1` installed already, it will get `mkldnn-v0.13`.

well, i can install `v0.14` with
```bash
conda install -c mingfeima mkldnn=0.14
```
But i want to make installing `v0.14` the default behavior, if the dependency (mkl) doesn't meet the requirement, i want to have it updated as also. What am i supposed to do here?

import pysam

When compiling vunit/vhdl/logging/src/log_types.vhd, Modelsim gives a warning on line 25 "Range 1 to 0 is null". Looking at the source, line 25 declares a constant of type log_level_vector_t. The log_level_vector_t is a natural array. Natural is 0 to integer'HIGH, so line 25 should be log_level_vector_t(0 to 1), instead of "1 to 0". When I make the change, the compiler warning goes away.

The support for snapshotting is phenomenal to the point where it's incredibly easy to automate.  It would be great if we could take advantage of this on a secondary cluster to perform automatic restoration. Something along the lines of:

```shell
$ curator restore --repository REPO_NAME snapshot --latest-snapshot
```

Under the hood, this should be able to simply grab the existing snapshots from that repository and find the newest one. The nicest part is that, as long as you end up with a single snapshot at the end, the `--latest-snapshot` option on the `snapshot` subcommand could simply be an augmentation rather than a requirement (e.g., it gives the latest of all returned options, so you could combine it with the other options, even including `--newer-than` and `--older-than`).

issue_title: Long running operations need to keep quiet.
issue_body:
Long-running operations such as vm start use PoSH style progress reporting, which is super annoying in any sort of real scenario.  

Consider
`az vm list -g <resource-group> --query "[].name" --output tsv | xargs -I {} -P 10 az vm start -g <resource-group> --vm-name "{}"`

You'll get a bizarre mish-mash of dots and gratuitous text.  The desired behavior is to have the command print nothing and return an appropriate error code when the operation completes

issue_title: Concerning the 0.37.0 release and library split
issue_body:
Dear community,

As you may have already [noticed](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/issues/333), we've been working on splitting the library into different packages. The split allows users to install only the service that they need, and also increase the maintainability of the library, which will allow faster releases in the future. These packages were just released under version 0.37.0:

azure-storage-blob
azure-storage-file
azure-storage-queue
azure-storage-common

You may notice that azure-storage-table is missing from the list, and that is because it’s now being released under a different namespace. Please refer to the new [Github repo](https://github.com/Azure/azure-cosmosdb-python) and [Pypi]( https://pypi.python.org/pypi/azure-cosmosdb-table) for more info. 

Anything that was previously directly under azure.storage are now in the new package azure.storage.common, which is a requirement for the new service packages(blob, file, queue). For example, azure.storage.CloudStorageAccount becomes azure.storage.common.CloudStorageAccount. The previous azure-storage meta-package is now deprecated after this change.

We understand that this is a significantly breaking change, but it is necessary in order to move forward. We welcome any feedback that you might have, and please refer to readme for more info on how to install the new packages. 😄

issue_title: Mapping to underlying VCR record modes is wrong
issue_body:
Currently `ReplayableTest.__init__` passes `self.config.record_mode` through unchanged as `record_mode` to `vcr.VCR()`. But it's a boolean value, which is not an appropriate value for that argument. It should be a string. Somehow it seems to work ok regardless? But I think it only does so incidentally.

To match what I believe we expect (and what I claim is done in #19), we should pass `record_mode` like this:
```python
record_mode='all' if self.config.record_mode else 'once'
```

issue_title: When downloading a directory that actually only has one file buried in it, the directory structure is not maintained
issue_body:
### Description
Using the ADLDownloader, when trying to download a directory with multiple subdirectories, if any of the lowest level folders have more than one file in them, the whole directory tree is downloaded as expected. However, if only one file actually exists in one of the lowest level subfolders, then the file is downloaded on its own as though it was only a single file specified for download, and not the whole directory. 

This is problematic when trying to programmatically interface with the ADL, pull down files to a VM, use them for computations, and then re-upload them when finished, as the expected file structure is not there for referencing file paths etc.

---

### Reproduction Steps
1. Connect to Azure Data lake Store using service to service application.
2. Download a subset of the ADL directory structure which may have many nested levels of subfolders, but only one of the lowest level subfolders actually has a file in it. Do this using eg. 
ADLDownloader(adlsFileSystemClient, rpath='RootFolder/Subfolder/Subfolder2', lpath='/localfolder', chunksize=1000000, buffersize=1000000, blocksize=1000000, client=None, run=True, overwrite=True, verbose=True)

Result: Only the single file is downloaded, it doesn't come within the subset of the file directory requested.. 

### Environment summary

**SDK Version:** What version of the SDK  are you using? (pip show azure-datalake-store)
Answer here: 0.0.19

**Python Version:** What Python version are you using? Is it 64-bit or 32-bit?  
Answer here: 3.6.2 64 bit

**OS Version:** What OS and version are you using?  
Answer here: Windows 7 Enterprise 64 bit

**Shell Type:** What shell are you using? (e.g. bash, cmd.exe, Bash on Windows)  
Answer here: Python with VS Code

## Long story short

Was trying to run the test on Python 3.5 and failed because I don't have IPv6 in a Docker container by default and the test thought I had.

root@bbbf05804213:/src# ip addr
1: lo: <LOOPBACK,UP,LOWER_UP> mtu 65536 qdisc noqueue state UNKNOWN group default qlen 1000

    link/ether 02:42:ac:11:00:02 brd ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff

       valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever
root@bbbf05804213:/src# python
Python 3.5.3 (default, Jul  8 2017, 05:09:47) 
[GCC 4.9.2] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import socket
>>> socket.AF_INET6
<AddressFamily.AF_INET6: 10>
```

## Expected behaviour

Use IPv4 if IPv6 is not present.

## Actual behaviour

According to the doc:
>  If the AF_UNIX constant is not defined then this protocol is unsupported. 

This probably means that the CPython has been compiled with IPv6, not that all the network interfaces has it.

## Steps to reproduce

```
# in aiohttp source directory
$ docker run -it --rm -v $PWD:/src -w /src python:3.5 /bin/bash
$ pip install tox
$ tox -e py35 -- -x tests/test_run_app.py
```

## Your environment

Python 3.5.3, Docker 17.05.0-ce build 89658bed64

Hi @asvetlov – thanks for another awesome aio library!

I was really excited to discover aiojobs as I believed it would allow me to clean up a lot of custom scheduling code I've written in my asyncio applications. Unfortunately, though, aiojobs doesn't actually support my single most-common use case: fire-and-forget background tasks.

I have lot of instances where I need to kick off an asynchronous background task *from synchronous code*. That usually ends up looking something like this:

```python
def some_sync_fn(*args, **kwargs):
    ...
    task = asyncio.ensure_future(some_async_fn('foo', 'bar', 123))
    ...
```

Obviously though this doesn't have the nice features provided by aiojobs. I got all excited to switch to aiojobs, but `Scheduler::spawn` is a coroutine – I can't call it from my sync code.

Well, that's not entirely accurate. `Scheduler::spawn` is *marked* `async`, but it never actually calls any async code:
https://github.com/aio-libs/aiojobs/blob/dd2cb0eae4d0295026b73d5fb843723aed84330a/aiojobs/_scheduler.py#L67-L81

The comment calls this out as an accommodation for possible future changes, but I would argue that this method should *never* be a coroutine. Making it so keeps consumers from scheduling background tasks from their sync code, which is (IMO at least) one of the major features a scheduler should offer. Is there something you have in mind for the future that requires `spawn` to be a coroutine?

Although I think it makes more sense for `spawn` to simply stop being a coroutine, I'd even settle for a different `Scheduler` method that sets up the task and returns immediately.

What do you think?

issue_title: Monitoring events may return None and unclean exit

# Description

I wanted to check something in particular: what happens if you only monitor events and the DOCKER_HOST is set to something unreachable.

# Current Behavior

I receive an invalid event, plus the script waits for nothing and if I interrupt it I get an unclean exit.

# Excepted Behavior

The script should not even start, it should notice that the connection cannot be made. Also it should exit cleanly.

# How To Reproduce

To do the test, I picked the example `events.py` and removed about everything except the loop:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import asyncio
from aiodocker.docker import Docker
from aiodocker.exceptions import DockerError


async def demo(docker):

    subscriber = docker.events.subscribe()

    while True:
        event = await subscriber.get()
        print(f"event: {event!r}")


if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    docker = Docker()
    try:
        # start a monitoring task.
        event_task = loop.create_task(docker.events.run())
        # do our stuffs.
        loop.run_until_complete(demo(docker))
        # explicitly stop monitoring.
        event_task.cancel()
    finally:
        loop.run_until_complete(docker.close())
        loop.close()
```

DOCKER_HOST=tcp://127.0.0.1:666 ./events.py

issue_title: Link to documentation in README.md is out of date
issue_body:
The README.md file says, at the end:

> Complete documentation, instructions, and examples are available at: https://www.messagebird.com/developers/python.

However, this link redirects to the generic https://developers.messagebird.com/ page, which is not Python-specific.

Either the link should be updated to point to Python-specific documentation, or it should point to the generic page (so that users aren't confused/frustrated at getting a generic page when they've been led to expect a Python-specific one).

Currently, redistribution of C libraries depends on platform-specific tools/packagers, and in the case where Anaconda isn't installed, obtaining a C library may be quite painful. Here is what I propose:

1. Generally, all C libraries should move to the CMake build system. This already happening thanks to the great work of @kitware.
2. Scikit-build should automatically package these dependendencies according to the following format:
   - scikit-build should be passed a git URL/revision directly in setup.py
   - scikit-build should clone/checkout such revision
   - scikit-build should build the project
   - scikit-build should copy the bin/ and /lib directories into the wheel
   - scikit-build should adjust these directories so that they are installed under `Python/Library`

To get a sense of what I'm going for, consider a directory with two files in it: `setup.cfg` and `pyproject.toml`:

```
    name='openblas',
    version='0.6.1',
    description='Improved build system generator for Python C extensions',
    author='The scikit-build team',
    author_email='scikit-build@googlegroups.com',
    url='https://github.com/scikit-build/scikit-build',
    source='gh:xianyi/OpenBLAS@v0.2.20'
    defines=[
        '-DDYNAMIC_ARCH=ON',
    ],
    license="MIT",
    zip_safe=False,
    keywords='scikit-build',
    classifiers=[
        'Development Status :: 2 - Pre-Alpha',
        'Intended Audience :: Developers',
        'License :: OSI Approved :: MIT License',
        'Natural Language :: English',
        "Programming Language :: Python :: 2",
        'Programming Language :: Python :: 2.7',
        'Programming Language :: Python :: 3',
        'Programming Language :: Python :: 3.3',
        'Programming Language :: Python :: 3.4',
        'Programming Language :: Python :: 3.5',
    ],
```

```
[build-system]
requires=['scikit-build']
# also need backend
```

These two files should, when `pip wheel .` is run, produce a wheel similar to the one obtained with `pip install mkl`, except of course the wheel would have OpenBLAS libraries. This feature would significantly reduce boilerplate and make repackaging C libraries much simpler.

issue_title: Subdomain lookups with `_resolver` URI when latest zonefile missing
issue_body:
Currently, _pending_ subdomain lookups require that the current zonefile for the parent domain be present to find the `_resolver` entry -- this leads to some issues when a zonefile hasn't propagated yet, but the UPDATE has confirmed.

This can be addressed by using the previous zonefile in these cases.

issue_title: Test the new UTXO provider with the registrar
issue_body:
Our (production) registrar currently uses a combination of BlockCypher and a custom bitcoind node (that watches certain addresses) for a UTXO provider. 

As we're moving to our own UTXO provider, we'll need to migrate the registrar as well for reliability etc.

Looking at #511 made me think we should set up an automated build process for our apt repository (for core, browser, etc.), as the apt installs really simplify the process of getting blockstack running on linux.

The blockstack/packaging repo already builds and deploys from a pretty straight-forward script, so this shouldn't be too difficult. We can integrate that into the Jenkins VM we have for running integration tests, and have it rebuild automatically on pushes to master branches (or be manual, but *less* manual than it is now)

Thoughts?

@jcnelson @jackzampolin

issue_title: Support for updating subdomain zonefile entries
issue_body:
Currently, our subdomain registrar only handles registration requests. It should additionally handle updating those entries. 

The resolver supports parsing and verifying these updates, but an endpoint needs to be added to the registrar (and verification code for the registrar, so that it doesn't add invalid entries to it's zonefiles, even if the resolvers will ultimately ignore them).

issue_title: Windows: coverage through multiprocessing not catched correctly
issue_body:
Hi ;)

Recently I'm trying to get the test coverage to 100% on a windows machine, but it doesn't want to. My code under test uses an event-loop driven by a `concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor`. Coverage is 100% on a Linux machine, but not on Windows.

PR:
https://github.com/coala/coala/pull/4475
Build on AppVeyor:
https://ci.appveyor.com/project/coala/coala/build/1.0.7774
Same build but on Linux (Travis):
https://travis-ci.org/coala/coala/builds/254572218?utm_source=github_status&utm_medium=notification
Relevant Python module/components under test:
https://github.com/coala/coala/tree/8f4113a2f471acf4c0121199c6ebb9b29dfa84ff/coalib/core, specificially
- https://github.com/coala/coala/blob/8f4113a2f471acf4c0121199c6ebb9b29dfa84ff/coalib/core/FileBear.py
- https://github.com/coala/coala/blob/8f4113a2f471acf4c0121199c6ebb9b29dfa84ff/coalib/core/ProjectBear.py

The missing lines are the ones inside `def execute_task`.

`setup.cfg` (and yes, coverage is enabled ;D):
https://github.com/coala/coala/blob/8f4113a2f471acf4c0121199c6ebb9b29dfa84ff/setup.cfg#L32

Exchanging the `ProcessPoolExecutor` with a `ThreadPoolExecutor` does the trick and produces full coverage. However it would be nice to have everything being tested with a `ProcessPoolExecutor` (especially due to possible pickling issues on Windows for subprocesses).

Not proven assumption:
I have the feeling that other parts of the code only run through a subprocess is measured properly, only those two modules under test don't work.

Background: the nbval plugin executes code cells from Jupyter notebooks as test cases. It would be very nice to have support for code coverage too.

Thank you for considering this.

issue_title: Some people's renewal spuriously appends digits to the directory URI
issue_body:
Described in

https://community.letsencrypt.org/t/renewal-failure-404-error/13193

Two different people are seeing 404 errors on renewal because their client is somehow appending a number (different for each of them) to the directory URI (despite these numbers seemingly *not* being present in the `server` variables in the renewal configs!).

I thought we saw something like this before, but the number was then actually present in the renewal config then, where in this case it appears not to be. I don't know why the client would modify the directory location in this way.

issue_title: Provide a way to run a script for setting up HTTP / DNS for validation
issue_body:
While a lot of sites can get by using the plug-ins shipped by letsencrypt (both existing and future ones), there will always be sites that require custom operations for provisioning the resources required for validation (e.g. files on a web server for Simple HTTP validation, DNS records for DNS validation). The easiest and most flexible way to automate this is running a user-provided script that gets passed the challenge type, identifiers (domains) and some challenge type specific parameters. E.g. for Simple HTTP validation, it could look like this:

`custom-provision-script --type simpleHttp --tls false --path .well-known/acme-challenge/Token1 --content '{ "type": "simpleHttp", "token": "Token1", "tls": false}' --domain example.com --path .well-known/acme-challenge/Token2 --content '{ "type": "simpleHttp", "token": "Token", "tls": false}' --domain example.com`

For DNS validation, it could look like this:

`custom-provision-script --type dns --name _acme-challenge --type txt --content Token1 --domain example.org --name _acme-challenge --type txt --content Token2 --domain example.com`

While those sites could write and use custom Authenticator plug-ins, scripts are easier to deploy. You  can invoke scripts with their full path, whereas letsencrypt plug-ins need to be installed using setuptools.

This has come up before (see e.g. #1015, #1475). While some users could make webroot work for them in combination with FUSE-based file systems (e.g. sshfs) or are satisfied with an interactive solution (using the manual plug-in), this isn't practical for many sites (including mine).
